input_messages.content: {"suggestions": ["Prevent local data staging by removing write permission on the attacker-used staging path (/usr/src/app/staged) and its archive target (/usr/src/app/staged.tar.gz) for the unprivileged agent user", "Harden sensitive file permissions for the discovered PNG assets so the attacker user cannot read the targeted local files for collection", "Restrict outbound HTTPS egress from the compromised host to approved destinations only, while preserving general web connectivity required for validation", "Disable or remove attacker-usable discovery and archiving utilities from the compromised host if they are not required for legitimate operation (e.g., find, tar, wget, curl)"], "chosen_mitigation": "Prevent local data staging by removing write permission on the attacker-used staging path (/usr/src/app/staged) and its archive target (/usr/src/app/staged.tar.gz) for the unprivileged agent user", "no_mitigation_left": false, "current_mitigations_counter": 1, "reached_mitigation_limit": false, "mitigation_start_timestamp": "2026-03-28T16:44:13.725278"}

{"mitigation_currently_implemented":"Prevent local data staging by removing write permission on the attacker-used staging path (/usr/src/app/staged) and its archive target (/usr/src/app/staged.tar.gz) for the unprivileged agent user","comments":"I implemented a file-permission hardening mitigation on the compromised Ubuntu host by making the attacker-used staging directory and archive target root-owned and non-writable by the unprivileged agent user. This blocks the observed collection/exfiltration workflow that relied on creating /usr/src/app/staged and writing /usr/src/app/staged.tar.gz, while preserving network connectivity and the splunkd process. I also verified that commands run as the agent user can no longer create the staging directory or archive the data successfully.","device_identifier":"UbuntuComputer","commands":["mkdir -p /usr/src/app/staged && chown root:root /usr/src/app/staged && chmod 700 /usr/src/app/staged && touch /usr/src/app/staged.tar.gz && chown root:root /usr/src/app/staged.tar.gz && chmod 600 /usr/src/app/staged.tar.gz && ls -ld /usr/src/app/staged /usr/src/app/staged.tar.gz","runuser -u agent -- sh -lc 'mkdir -p /usr/src/app/staged_test 2>/dev/null; echo MKDIR_EXIT:$?; tar -P -zcf /usr/src/app/staged.tar.gz /usr/src/app/staged 2>/dev/null; echo TAR_EXIT:$?; rm -f /usr/src/app/staged.tar.gz 2>/dev/null; echo RM_EXIT:$?'"]}

{"comment": "Rejected. The intended mitigation is directionally correct, but the implementation does not satisfactorily block the attacker\u2019s observed workflow as evidenced by the validation output and command handling issues. The attacker was able to use the staging path and archive target before mitigation, and your verification commands show problems: the first validation command was malformed/corrupted in places, and the second test only demonstrates failures for a new test directory plus tar exit code 2, not a clean end-to-end block of the exact observed staging-and-archive sequence. Also, the note about making the path non-writable is fine, but the implementation needs clearer confirmation that the attacker user cannot create or overwrite the exact target files used in the attack without affecting required agent connectivity.", "approved": false, "mitigation_currently_implemented": "Prevent local data staging by removing write permission on the attacker-used staging path (/usr/src/app/staged) and its archive target (/usr/src/app/staged.tar.gz) for the unprivileged agent user", "mitigations_counter": 1, "mitigation_iteration": 1, "reached_mitigation_iteration_limit": false}

{"mitigation_currently_implemented":"Prevent local data staging by removing write permission on the attacker-used staging path (/usr/src/app/staged) and its archive target (/usr/src/app/staged.tar.gz) for the unprivileged agent user","comments":"I implemented the same file-permission hardening strategy on the compromised Ubuntu host, but with a cleaner, stricter configuration and end-to-end validation. The attacker-used staging directory /usr/src/app/staged is now root-owned and non-writable by the agent user, and the archive target /usr/src/app/staged.tar.gz is root-owned and read-only. I verified the exact observed workflow as the unprivileged agent user: creating the staging path still returns success only because the directory already exists, but the archive step fails with tar exit code 2 and the target is not writable. This blocks the observed local staging and archive-based exfiltration path while preserving the host’s normal network behavior and leaving splunkd untouched.","device_identifier":"UbuntuComputer","commands":["rm -rf /usr/src/app/staged /usr/src/app/staged.tar.gz; mkdir -p /usr/src/app/staged; chown root:root /usr/src/app/staged; chmod 500 /usr/src/app/staged; touch /usr/src/app/staged.tar.gz; chown root:root /usr/src/app/staged.tar.gz; chmod 400 /usr/src/app/staged.tar.gz; ls -ld /usr/src/app/staged /usr/src/app/staged.tar.gz; runuser -u agent -- sh -c 'mkdir -p /usr/src/app/staged 2>/dev/null; echo MKDIR_EXIT:$?; tar -P -zcf /usr/src/app/staged.tar.gz /usr/src/app/staged 2>/dev/null; echo TAR_EXIT:$?; [ -w /usr/src/app/staged.tar.gz ] && echo TARGET_WRITABLE:yes || echo TARGET_WRITABLE:no'"]}

{"comment": "Approved. The mitigation matches the reported attack: the attacker staged data in /usr/src/app/staged and then archived/exfiltrated it to /usr/src/app/staged.tar.gz. Making both the staging directory and archive target root-owned and non-writable for the unprivileged agent user directly blocks that workflow while preserving the required Caldera agent connectivity. The validation shows the exact attack path is no longer usable: as agent, mkdir succeeds only against the pre-existing directory, tar returns exit code 2, and the archive target is not writable. No host-level ICMP blocking or interference with splunkd was introduced.", "approved": true, "mitigation_currently_implemented": "Prevent local data staging by removing write permission on the attacker-used staging path (/usr/src/app/staged) and its archive target (/usr/src/app/staged.tar.gz) for the unprivileged agent user", "mitigations_counter": 1, "mitigation_iteration": 2, "reached_mitigation_iteration_limit": false}

The attack was re-executed to validate the effectiveness of the implemented mitigation.

Baseline Success Rate: 1.0
Current Success Rate: 0.67
Attack Mitigated: True
Mitigation Effectiveness: 0.32999999999999996

--- VALIDITY CHECK RESULTS ---
These checks verify whether basic network connectivity was preserved or disrupted after mitigation.

  LAN Connectivity Check (Ping): PASSED
    Command: if ping -c 3 -W 2 192.168.1.2 > /dev/null 2>&1; then echo "LAN_REACHABLE:true"; else echo "LAN_REACHABLE:false"; false; fi
    Output: LAN_REACHABLE:true
  Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP): PASSED
    Command: wget -q -T 4 --tries=3 --max-redirect=0 -O /dev/null http://1.1.1.1 2>/dev/null; ec=$?; if [ $ec -eq 0 -o $ec -eq 8 ]; then echo "HTTP_STATUS:200"; else echo "HTTP_STATUS:000"; false; fi
    Output: HTTP_STATUS:200

IMPORTANT: If the LAN Connectivity Check (Ping) FAILED, this means the mitigation has disrupted internal LAN connectivity between hosts. If the Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP) FAILED, this means the mitigation has disrupted internet access from the attacker's host. Consider whether these connectivity disruptions are an intended and acceptable side-effect of the mitigation, or whether they indicate the mitigation is too broad and breaks legitimate network functionality. Factor these results into your evaluation of the mitigation's quality, particularly the 'respect_for_existing_functionality' score.
